segments.text: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. I am Mayank and today Varun and I am going to talk to you
through the story behind this session, the most expensive connector. This is really
a talk about what happens when a successful integration grows beyond what it was originally
designed for. We will say how that happened to us, what we learned from customers and how
it boosts us towards a much more scalable architecture. Before we start, a quick intro.
I am Mayank, Senior Software Engineer at Autodesk and I am joined by Varun. Varun, please introduce
yourself. Thank you, thank you, Mayank for inviting me. I am Varun, co-founder and CEO of
EncircleTech, been in this industry for about 20 years now. I am happy to be part of this call.
We are coming at this from slightly different angles, which actually makes this a stronger
story because what we are sharing today comes from a real product building, real customer
adoption and real scaling problems. I will cover how the problem evolved and why we
changed the architecture. Then Varun will walk through the back end, the demo and what
this design makes possible in practice. This is the flow for today. We will start with
the connector story and the customer pressure that changed the problem for us. Then we
will walk you through the architecture shift from thick client to thin client and briefly touch
on SSA. After that, Varun will take over and show the smart back end, the demo and the bigger
set of use cases. This architecture unlocks. So the talk moves from problem to redesign to
prove to what comes next. We saved the power BI connector for data exchange and honestly,
customers loved it. It solved a real lead, it helped teams pull design data into dashboards
and start getting real value quickly. But then something interesting happened. People didn't
use it lightly, they pushed it hard, they loved, they started loading much bigger models,
they started using the data for real business decisions and very quickly they stopped
trading it like a simple connector. They wanted real time, they wanted scale and that was the
moment we realized. This was no longer just a connector problem. It had became a data volume problem
because when you start thinking about AI analytics, live dashboards and automation, the challenge
changes. It's not about making one API call, it is not about handling 100 records, not 1000,
not even a million. In the AI era, the real question is can your architecture survive the volume?
That become our turning point. So this talk is really the story of what happened next.
How we moved from building a connector that could make cause to building an architecture that
could handle scale. This is where we started and to be fair, it was a good place to start. This
connector was already in the market, it had real customers, real adoption and real value.
Architecture forms were using it to bring rapid data into power BI dashboards.
Construction teams were using it to track model changes and monitor project in much easier way.
So this was not a lab idea, it was not a prototype, it was something people were actually using
using in their day to day work and that matters because customer success is usually the best
test of whether you build something useful but success also changes expectations.
Once customers saw the value, they wanted to do more with it, more data, more models, more frequent
refreshes, more business decisions based on what they were seeing in those dashboards.
And that was the tip. What started as a useful connector was now being asked to support much
bigger, more serious use cases. So yes, it worked. But customers were growing faster than the
architecture behind it and that is really where the next part of the story begins.
On the previous slide, I saw that the connector was already working and customers were getting
real value from it. But once customers started depending on it, the conversation changed.
They were no longer asking for small features. They were hitting real limits.
Some were telling us that loading last property set was too slow. Others were saying that their
dashboards were now tied to real business decisions so the data had to be much more current.
Some teams were rebuilding the same integration again and again for every customer.
And more and more customers wanted this data to go beyond dashboards into AI pipelines,
fabric and broader enterprises workflow. So taken one by one, these might sound like small
separate requests but for us, they all pointed to the same thing. The connector had been adopted
successfully and now customer needed it to grow with them. And honestly, this is a good problem to
have because it means customer believed in the value. They were not asking should we use this.
They were asking can this support where we are going next? This is a very different kind of question
and it forced us to step back and realize this was not about turning one more feature inside the
connector. This was the movement we had to rethink the entire architecture itself.
Once we understood the customer pressure clearly, we knew this was not a feature problem anymore.
It was an architecture problem. Customers were asking the connector to support bigger data,
more frequent references and more serious business use cases. So instead of adding more and more
logic into the same design, we stopped and asked in more important question.
Is the connector really the right place for all this complexity? That question led to the
biggest shift we made. Our answer was no. The connector should not carry all that weight.
Before the connector was doing a lot, authentication, pagination, transforms, orchestration,
all inside the client. That helped us move fast in the beginning but over time, it became clear
that this approach would not scale. With the way customers were using this product.
So we changed it. We made the connector thin. Its job became much simpler, fetch and display.
Then we moved the heavy work into a smart backend, parallel fetches, streaming, parkage generation,
all handled in a place that is much easier to scale and reuse.
Instead of asking the connector to be smart, heavy and complex, we let it stay lightweight and
we put the intelligence where it can actually scale. So in a simple way, the shift was less
intelligence in the connector, more capability in the backend. And that gave us a much stronger
foundation for everything that comes next. And once we made that shift, we could clearly describe
the three big changes that came out of it. So if I had to reduce the whole design into three
changes, it would be these. First, we moved all the heavy processing from client into a backend
that we controlled. That was important because the connector had gradually become responsible
for too much. It was doing more than just connecting. It was starting to carry logic that
really belonged somewhere more scalable. Second, the backend now handles streaming. So the connector
can return data much closer to real time. That is direct response of what customers were telling us.
They did not want static snapsaw. They wanted fresher data. They could actually use for
decisions. And third, we replaced in memory transforms with streaming parking generation.
That gave us much better path for handling large models, data volumes without overloading the client.
So the simple summary is this. We moved the brain out of the connector into the backend.
The connector became lighter. The backend became smarter. And that gave us much stronger base
for scale. As a part of this bigger conversation around scalable architecture, we also wanted to
briefly call out SS. It is an important capability for customers building backend based integrations
on APS. The idea is pretty simple. Instead of every user authenticating one by one,
an admin can delegate access to a backend service for specific projects and files.
That means your backend can handle the work in a more controlled and scalable way.
Why this is useful? Because as soon as your architecture starts moving beyond a simple client flow,
authentication can become a real source of complexity. And SS gives you a cleaner model for that.
It is booked. It is explicit. It is allatable. And it can be removed.
So this is not about bypassing the admin. It's actually about making backend access more manageable
while keeping the admin in control. It is very useful capability to be aware of.
Especially if you are building the kind of backend driven architecture we are talking about here.
With that context, I will hand it over to Varun who will walk you through the smart backend itself
because that is where most of the scale and performance gain came from. Over to Varun.
Thank you, man. So with that, friends, let me walk you through the smart backend that
Mayang has been talking about. Essentially, the big shift that we did was moving away from
individual API calls pagination for 1000 Reconcert at a time to a single large
parquet file streaming over the server. So this is how it works. You can have any kind of client,
Power BI client, tab you any custom app, which is initiating a request with the server.
Now this is queued on the server so that even if multiple users, multiple dashboards are making a

Essentially, we are processing requests. First in first out, streaming the parquet files, which can
then handle about millions of records in single go. It is streaming. It doesn't increase your memory
limits. These kind of job IDs are stored also in DynamoDB for future tracking and referencing,
which then enables us to resume or provide updated data on the same job ID.
What this enables is essentially all those earlier architecture where you're constantly making
parallel API calls is now replaced with one call streaming with no memory limits. It's much
faster and it can support much larger models. We are able to, we don't need to now download the
entire 3D model. We are connecting it live and loading data much faster as a 3D content in your
dashboards. The parquet files are really the backbone of many systems including Microsoft fabric.
